criteria: The agent has read Section 2 (Preliminaries) completely to understand the decision tree ensemble model, including the definitions of leaf nodes, internal nodes, guards, and the three output notions (raw, prob, label).
The agent has read Section 3 to understand the (g, F)-sensitivity definition (Definition 3.1) and the NP-hardness result for depth-1 tree ensembles (Theorem 3.2).

The agent has read Section 4 completely to understand the data-aware sensitivity framework, including the extended (g, F, u)-sensitivity definition (Definition 4.1), the marginal probability utility function, and the clause-summary cavity-pruning approach.
The agent has read Section 5 (base MILP encoding) to understand the predicate variables p_fk, leaf variables l_n, and base constraints Equations (2)–(6) and Gap-bin.
The agent has read Section 5.1 to understand the unaffected-leaf optimization (Equation UnAff) and the affected-leaf constraint (Equation Aff-bin).
The agent has read Section 5.2 to understand the MILP objective function optimization (Equation Obj-bin) and its role in guiding the solver.
The agent has read Section 5.3 to understand both data-aware modifications: the log-sum utility objective (Equation 7) and the SMT-based clause-summary approach for pruning data-sparse regions.
The agent has read Section 6 to understand the multiclass extension, including Definition 6.1 for (g, F, c1, c2)-sensitivity and the modified constraints Gap-multi, Aff-multi, and Obj-multi.

The agent has read Table 1 to understand all benchmark details: 10 binary classifiers (models 1–10) and 8 multiclass classifiers (models 1–8) with their respective tree counts, depths, and feature/class counts.
The agent has read Figure 3 to check the cactus-plot runtime results for binary classification (ENSENSE vs KANT vs SENSPB) and multiclass classification (ENSENSE vs KANT).

The agent has read Figure 4 to understand the data-aware quality evaluation: the mean l2 distance metric, pairwise win/draw/loss comparisons, and the cactus plot for the four methods (None, Prob, Clause, Probclause).

The agent has read Appendix G.1 to understand XGBoost training details: version v1.7.1, binary:logistic objective, label encoding, missing-value removal, 20% validation split with seed=42, and hyperparameter grid over max_depth and boosting rounds.
The agent has read Appendix G.2 to understand the ablation study design, which ablates UnAff, Aff-bin, and Obj-bin individually and in combination for both binary and multiclass settings (Figure 6).
The agent has read Appendix G.3 to understand the multi-feature sensitivity experiments with |F| = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 on binary classifiers (Figure 7).

The plan includes parsing or loading XGBoost tree ensemble models and extracting the tree structure: internal nodes with guards (X_f < tau), leaf nodes with values, and the tree/leaf partition for each class in the multiclass setting.

The plan includes constructing the base MILP encoding with binary predicate variables p_fk for each feature guard, continuous leaf variables l_n, and the constraint system from Equations (2)–(6) and Gap-bin.
The plan includes identifying unaffected leaves (whose entire ancestry does not involve any feature in F) and implementing the UnAff and Aff-bin optimization constraints.
The plan includes adding the Obj-bin objective function (MAX sum of difference of weighted leaf values) to guide the MILP solver.
The plan includes computing the per-feature marginal probability function pi_f by counting dataset points in each guard-induced interval and normalizing by the dataset size.
The plan includes replacing the Obj-bin objective with the log-sum utility objective (Equation 7) to implement the Prob data-aware variant.

The plan includes using an SMT solver (z3) to iteratively compute clause summaries of bounded boxes (cavities) in the training data, guided by a tightness objective and counterexample-guided refinement.
The plan includes adding synthesized clause constraints (negations of cavity boxes) to the MILP encoding to avoid sensitivity pairs in data-sparse regions.
The plan includes extending the MILP encoding to multiclass sensitivity by modifying Gap-bin to Gap-multi, Aff-bin to Aff-multi, and Obj-bin to Obj-multi, using log(g) = eta as the gap parameter.

The plan includes training XGBoost models on all UCI benchmark datasets (adult, churn, pimadiabetes, german_credit) with the specified hyperparameter grid (max_depth in {5,6}, boosting rounds in {200,300,500} or {500,800}), using Gurobi as the MILP solver.

The plan includes designing experiments to compare ENSENSE (full system) against SENSPB and KANT for binary classification, and against KANT for multiclass classification, on all benchmark instances with gap in {0.5, 1, 1.5} and a 3600-second per-instance timeout.
The plan includes implementing the l2 distance metric from training data to the nearest counterexample region (not just a point) by computing the closest axis-aligned polytope face.

The plan includes designing the ablation study to test all six combinations of the three optimizations (None, Obj, Aff+Obj, UnAff+Obj, UnAff+Aff, UnAff+Aff+Obj) for both binary and multiclass settings.
The plan includes the multi-feature sensitivity experiments with |F| in {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} using randomly sampled feature subsets on binary benchmarks.

The code for loading and parsing XGBoost tree ensembles has been implemented, correctly extracting all internal-node guards (feature index f, threshold tau_fk), leaf values (n.val), and tree partitions for multiclass models.

The code for enumerating all guard thresholds per feature and assigning sorted predicate variable indices p_fk has been implemented, with sorted thresholds tau_f1 < ... < tau_fKf and boundary guards X_f < -inf and X_f < inf.
The MILP predicate monotonicity constraint (Equation 2: p_f1 <= p_f2 <= ... <= p_fKf) has been implemented for each feature f across both copies of the input variables.
The MILP leaf exclusivity constraint (Equation 3: l_1 + l_2 + ... + l_N = 1) has been implemented for each tree in the ensemble for both input copies.
The MILP leaf-to-predicate consistency constraint for root nodes (Equation 4: 1 - sum_{n in FSet} l_n = p_fk = sum_{n in TSet} l_n) has been implemented.
The MILP leaf-to-predicate consistency constraint for non-root nodes (Equation 5: 1 - sum_{n in FSet} l_n >= p_fk >= sum_{n in TSet} l_n) has been implemented.
The MILP feature-equality constraint (Equation 6) has been implemented, requiring p_fk^(1) = p_fk^(2) for all predicate variables whose guard feature is NOT in the sensitive feature set F.
The Gap-bin constraint has been implemented: sum_{n in A} l_n^(1) * n.val >= delta AND sum_{n in A} l_n^(2) * n.val <= -delta, where delta = SIGMOID^{-1}(0.5 + g).
The code for identifying the set U of unaffected leaves (leaves whose entire root-to-leaf path ancestry contains only guards on features not in F) has been implemented.
The UnAff constraint (l_n^(1) = l_n^(2) for all l_n in U) has been added to the MILP encoding.
The Aff-bin constraint (sum_{l_n not in U} (l_n^(1) * n.val - l_n^(2) * n.val) >= 2 * delta) has been implemented as a tighter replacement that only involves affected leaves.
The Obj-bin objective function (MAX sum_{n in A} (l_n^(1) * n.val - l_n^(2) * n.val)) has been implemented to guide the MILP solver toward feasible solutions.

The per-feature marginal probability function pi_f(v) has been implemented by counting dataset points D in each guard-induced interval [tau_f(k-1), tau_fk) and dividing by |D|, matching Equation (1) from Section 4.
The product-of-marginals utility function u(x^(1), x^(2)) = pi(x^(1)) * pi(x^(2)) has been implemented as the product of per-feature marginals, assuming feature independence.

The log-sum data-aware objective (Equation 7) has been implemented: MAX sum_{f in F} sum_{k=2}^{Kf} (log(pi_f(tau_f(k-1))) - log(pi_f(tau_fk))) * (p_fk^(1) + p_fk^(2)), converting the product-of-marginals to an additive MILP objective.

The SMT clause synthesis has been implemented using z3 to find data cavities as bounded box conjunctions of the form AND_{i in [1,w]} (X_fi >= lb_i AND X_fi < ub_i), with Boolean variables r_if, s_ik, t_ik for feature and bound selection.

The SMT clause synthesis includes a tightness objective (MIN sum of interval widths) and counterexample-guided refinement that adds constraints to exclude previously found clauses, iteratively computing one cavity clause at a time.

The greedy literal-pruning step has been implemented to minimize each synthesized cavity clause (removing redundant literals) without reducing data coverage, with discarding of clauses that enclose any training point.

The computed cavity clauses are added to the MILP as constraints requiring any counterexample pair to satisfy the negation of each cavity (i.e., at least one input is outside the cavity box), with a limit of 1500 clauses.

The multiclass Gap-multi constraint has been implemented: for each c != c^(1), sum_{ln in L_c(1)} l_n^(1) * n.val > sum_{ln in Lc} l_n^(1) * n.val + eta, and symmetrically for c^(2), where eta = ln(g).

The multiclass Aff-multi constraint has been implemented: sum_{ln in L_c(1), n not in U} (l_n^(1) * n.val - l_n^(2) * n.val) + sum_{ln in L_c(2), n not in U} (l_n^(2) * n.val - l_n^(1) * n.val) > 2 * eta.
The multiclass Obj-multi objective function has been implemented: MAX sum_{ln in L_c(1)} (l_n^(1) * n.val - l_n^(2) * n.val) + sum_{ln in L_c(2)} (l_n^(2) * n.val - l_n^(1) * n.val).

The ENSENSE tool has been implemented integrating the full MILP encoding (with all optimizations: UnAff, Aff-bin, Obj-bin) and the two data-aware strategies (Prob, Clause) into a single tool using Gurobi as the MILP solver and z3 as the SMT solver.

The code for loading the pre-trained binary classification benchmarks (models 1–6 from Chen et al. 2019b and models 7–10 trained on UCI datasets: adult, churn, pimadiabetes, german_credit) has been implemented.
The code for loading the multiclass classification benchmarks (covtype, fashion, ori_mnist, Iris, Red-Wine with their specified tree counts, depths, and class counts) has been implemented.

The XGBoost model training code for UCI binary datasets has been implemented with the correct configuration: v1.7.1, binary:logistic objective, label encoding for categoricals, missing-value row removal, 20% validation split (seed=42), and hyperparameter grid over max_depth in {5,6} and boosting rounds in {200,300,500} for benchmarks 7–9 and {500,800} for benchmark 10.

The l2 distance evaluation metric has been implemented: computing the l2 distance over insensitive features between each training data point and the nearest counterexample region (not a single witness point), where the counterexample region is the axis-aligned polytope defined by the joint leaf-assignment pattern.
The SENSPB baseline (pseudo-Boolean encoding from Ahmad et al. 2025) has been integrated or re-implemented to enable comparison with ENSENSE on binary classification benchmarks.

The KANT baseline (Kantchelian et al. 2016 MILP encoding without the UnAff, Aff-bin, or Obj-bin optimizations) has been implemented for comparison against ENSENSE in both binary and multiclass settings.

The clause-size schedule has been implemented: clauses of width w are enumerated starting from w=1 and progressively increasing to a user-defined maximum, with the feature ordering constraint (i < j => f_i < f_j) enforced to avoid redundant clauses.
The code for multi-feature sensitivity has been implemented, allowing |F| > 1 by sampling random feature subsets of size m from the feature set for each benchmark instance.

The binary classification sensitivity experiments comparing ENSENSE against KANT and SENSPB have been executed successfully on all 1,290 benchmark instances (10 binary models, single-feature, gap in {0.5, 1, 1.5}), with a 3600-second per-instance timeout using a single CPU core.

The multiclass classification sensitivity experiments comparing ENSENSE against KANT have been executed successfully on all 538 benchmark instances (8 multiclass models, single-feature, gap in {0.5, 1, 1.5}), with a 3600-second per-instance timeout.

The data-aware sensitivity experiments comparing four methods (None, Prob, Clause, Probclause) have been executed successfully on the binary classification benchmarks with gap in {0.5, 1, 1.5}, producing l2 distance results for each method.

The ablation study experiments testing all six optimization combinations (None, Obj, Aff+Obj, UnAff+Obj, UnAff+Aff, UnAff+Aff+Obj) have been executed successfully for both binary and multiclass benchmarks, producing cactus plot timing data.
The multi-feature sensitivity experiments with |F| in {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} have been executed successfully on binary classification benchmarks (430 total instances), producing timing results.

The XGBoost model training has been executed successfully for all UCI binary benchmark configurations, producing 6 model variants per dataset (all combinations of max_depth in {5,6} and boosting rounds in {200,300,500}) for benchmarks 7–9 and 2 variants for benchmark 10.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3(a): ENSENSE achieves approximately 8x average speedup over KANT on binary classification benchmarks, with no timeouts for ENSENSE while KANT times out on 153 instances.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3(a): ENSENSE achieves approximately 5x average speedup over SENSPB on binary classification benchmarks, with no timeouts for ENSENSE while SENSPB times out on 205 instances.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3(b): ENSENSE achieves approximately 15x average speedup over KANT on multiclass classification benchmarks, with no timeouts for ENSENSE while KANT times out on 256 instances.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: ENSENSE substantially outperforms VERITAS on most multiclass datasets (covtype_robust, covtype_unrobust, fashion_robust, fashion_unrobust, ori_mnist_robust, ori_mnist_unrobust), where VERITAS finds near-zero gap (0%) while ENSENSE produces correct SAT results within the timeout.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: The Probclause method achieves the best data-aware quality, winning 86.7% of pairwise comparisons against the None (baseline) method with a mean l2 distance advantage of 0.47 on wins.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: The Clause method wins 80.59% of pairwise comparisons against None, and the Prob method wins 76.65% against None, with mean l2 distances of 0.306 (Clause) and 0.26 (Prob) versus 0.57 (None).
The execution outcomes match Figure 4: The Probclause method achieves the lowest mean l2 distance of 0.17 across all instances, compared to 0.57 (None), 0.26 (Prob), and 0.306 (Clause).

The execution outcomes match Figure 6 (ablation): The full optimization (UnAff+Aff+Obj) achieves the best runtime performance in binary classification, with each constraint contributing to overall improvement and an order-of-magnitude reduction in timeouts compared to no optimization.

The execution outcomes match Figure 6 (ablation): In multiclass classification, the full optimization (UnAff+Aff+Obj) achieves the best runtime performance, with the Aff-bin constraint contributing most significantly such that adding UnAff on top provides minimal additional benefit.

The execution outcomes match Figure 7: In multi-feature sensitivity, ENSENSE's performance improves (runtime decreases) as the number of varying features |F| increases from 1 to 5, because the search space over insensitive features shrinks.
